title: Cosine Decay
concept: Cosine decay lowers the learning rate smoothly over time, helping training shift from large moves to fine adjustments without abrupt drops.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In step decay, how does the learning rate usually change?

options.text: In clear stages with drops at milestones.
By rewriting the dataset after every epoch.
By turning gradients off near the end.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Step decay is a staircase, not a smooth glide.

cards.title: Dimmer switch, not light switch
cards.body: A room can be darkened with a sudden click, or with a dimmer that fades the light gently. Cosine decay behaves more like the dimmer: the learning rate eases downward instead of falling off a cliff.

cards.title: A smooth gradual fall
cards.body: With cosine decay, the learning rate starts higher and then decreases smoothly as training continues. Instead of a few sharp milestone drops, the step size gradually fades, which can make the end of training feel calmer and less jerky.

cards.title: Like a sunset
cards.body: Sunlight does not usually vanish in one giant jump. It fades from bright afternoon to dim evening. Cosine decay gives training that same smooth transition from energetic updates to gentle finishing steps.

cards.title: A long run with less hand-tuning
cards.body: Suppose you plan a long training run for a deep network. Rather than choosing several exact milestone epochs by hand, cosine decay can keep shrinking the learning rate smoothly so the model naturally moves from exploration toward careful polishing.

cards.title: Smooth does not mean weak
cards.body: A common myth is that a smooth schedule must be timid from the start. Not true. Cosine decay can still begin at a useful learning rate; it simply reduces that rate more gracefully over time.

cards.title: Cliffs vs. glide path
cards.body: Cosine decay and step decay both get smaller over time, but they travel there differently.

left.text:
Long flat plateaus.
Sudden drops at checkpoints.
Very explicit milestones.

right.text:
A smooth downward slide.
No sharp cliffs.
Gentle transition into fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: Why do some teams prefer cosine decay over step decay?

options.text: Because it lowers the learning rate smoothly instead of in abrupt jumps.
Because it removes the need for gradients.
Because it works only on datasets with no labels.
Because it increases the number of hidden layers automatically.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The appeal is the smooth glide from larger updates to smaller ones.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Cosine decay usually decreases the learning rate smoothly rather than through a few sudden milestone drops.
exercises.explain: True. Smooth change is the whole point of cosine decay.

exercises.sentence: Compared with step decay, cosine decay gives a more ___ drop in the learning rate.
exercises.explain: Correct. Cosine decay trades staircase drops for a gradual fade.

exercises.prompt: Which training story sounds most like cosine decay?

options.text: The learning rate gently shrinks across the run so the model settles more smoothly near the end.
The learning rate stays frozen forever with no changes.
The learning rate only changes when the dataset gains new labels.

exercises.explain: Right. Cosine decay is about a smooth slide, not a staircase or a fixed flat line.

exercises.prompt: A team wants a learning rate schedule for a long transformer training run. They do not want to hand-pick many milestone epochs, and they want the step size to fade smoothly into the final fine-tuning phase. Which choice best matches that goal?

options.text: Use cosine decay so the learning rate gradually falls over time.
Keep one fixed learning rate forever because long runs never need adaptation.
Raise the learning rate sharply near the end so the model overshoots more.
Ignore schedules and replace the labels every epoch.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Cosine decay fits when you want a smooth finish without managing a bunch of discrete milestones.
